old_value:
{"id":"nayra-0h9","title":"Fix wasted vertical space: move axis from center to bottom-anchored position","description":"## Problem

The timeline axis is hardcoded at `height / 2` (renderer.js:250), placing it dead center of the canvas. Since event lanes only grow upward from the axis, and time labels sit 30px below it, the bottom ~45% of the canvas is always empty. For sparse/single-lane datasets like Human Era, the visible content is a 20px strip in the middle of the screen.

## Root cause

```js
// renderer.js:250
const axisY = height / 2;  // ← always dead center
```

```js
// lane-positioning.js:38
return axisY - laneHeight/2 - offset;  // ← lanes only go UP
```

```js
// renderer.js:646
ctx.fillText(label, x, axisY + 30);  // ← labels 30px below axis
```

## Fix

Move axis to near the bottom:

```js
const AXIS_BOTTOM_MARGIN = 60; // room for labels + padding
const axisY = height - AXIS_BOTTOM_MARGIN;
```

This makes all lanes grow upward into the full canvas height instead of only the top half.

## Acceptance criteria

- Axis is positioned near the bottom of the canvas (configurable margin)
- For the Human Era example, events use the majority of vertical canvas space
- Grid lines, hit detection, spatial hash, and label placement all remain correct
- Existing tests pass","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"charly.vibes@proton.me","created_at":"2026-03-03T20:21:33Z","created_by":"charly vibes","updated_at":"2026-03-03T20:21:33Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"## Problem

The timeline axis is hardcoded at `height / 2` (renderer.js:250), placing it dead center of the canvas. Since event lanes only grow upward from the axis, and time labels sit 30px below it, the bottom ~45% of the canvas is always empty. For sparse/single-lane datasets like Human Era, the visible content is a 20px strip in the middle of the screen.

## Root cause

```js
// renderer.js:250
const axisY = height / 2;  // ← always dead center
```

```js
// lane-positioning.js:38
return axisY - laneHeight/2 - offset;  // ← lanes only go UP
```

```js
// renderer.js:646
ctx.fillText(label, x, axisY + 30);  // ← labels 30px below axis
```

## Fix

Export a named constant and helper from renderer.js:

```js
// Must be > 30px (label offset below axis) to keep labels on-screen
export const AXIS_BOTTOM_MARGIN = 60;
export function getAxisY(height) { return height - AXIS_BOTTOM_MARGIN; }
```

Then use it in all three places that independently compute axisY:

| File | Line(s) | Change |
|---|---|---|
| renderer.js | 250 | `const axisY = height / 2` → `const axisY = getAxisY(height)` |
| hit-detection.js | 10, 73 | `const axisY = canvasHeight / 2` → import + `getAxisY(canvasHeight)` |
| test/unit/hit-detection.test.js | 17 | `const axisY = canvasHeight / 2` → import + `getAxisY(canvasHeight)` |

hit-detection.js already imports EVENT_HEIGHT and getClusters from renderer.js so no new dependency is introduced.

## Out of scope

- Lane overflow clipping (events rendering above y=0 when many lanes exist) — separate issue
- e2e tests in event-info.spec.js click at height/2 but use soft assertions (`if visible`) so they will not fail

## Acceptance criteria

- `AXIS_BOTTOM_MARGIN` is a named export from renderer.js (not a magic number)
- `getAxisY(height)` is a named export from renderer.js used everywhere axisY is calculated
- For Human Era example, events occupy the majority of vertical canvas space instead of a thin center strip
- Hit detection and spatial hash remain correct after the change
- `npm test` passes","status":"open"}

Then use it in all three places that independently compute axisY:

| File | Line(s) | Change |
|---|---|---|

hit-detection.js already imports EVENT_HEIGHT and getClusters from renderer.js so no new dependency is introduced.

{"title":"Use selected event as zoom anchor instead of mouse position"}
old_value: {"id":"nayra-9xe","title":"Zoom centers on selected event instead of mouse position","description":"When a user selects/clicks an event on the timeline, subsequent zoom operations should center around that event rather than the mouse cursor position. This improves usability because after selecting an event of interest, zooming in should reveal more detail about that event rather than whatever happens to be under the mouse. The event's center point (or start point) should become the zoom anchor when an event is selected.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"charly.vibes@proton.me","created_at":"2026-03-03T20:56:20Z","created_by":"charly vibes","updated_at":"2026-03-03T20:56:20Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"When an event is selected on the timeline, pressing Space or Enter should trigger the same action as clicking the event (e.g. opening its detail panel, following its URL, or whatever the primary click action is). This follows common UI conventions (Space/Enter activate the focused element) and enables fully keyboard-driven navigation.

Acceptance criteria:
- Pressing SPC or ENTER with an event selected triggers the primary click action on that event
- No action if nothing is selected
- Works consistently across all example timelines

Verification: Select an event with mouse click, then press SPC or ENTER — result should match double-clicking the event."}
old_value: {"id":"nayra-1l2","title":"SPC and ENTER open selected event","description":"When an event is selected on the timeline, pressing Space or Enter should open/activate it — the same action as clicking/double-clicking the event. This follows common UI conventions (Space/Enter to activate the focused element) and makes keyboard-only navigation fully functional.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"charly.vibes@proton.me","created_at":"2026-03-03T20:58:46Z","created_by":"charly vibes","updated_at":"2026-03-03T20:58:46Z"}

new_value:
{"description":"In the multilane example, event bars show symbols/icons (emojis or unicode characters) that appear scrambled together with the event title text. For example 'Frontend Foundation' renders as 'rontend Foundation' — the leading emoji/symbol displaces the first character of the text.

Likely cause: emoji characters have different measured widths in canvas vs actual rendered width (especially on different platforms), causing the text x-offset calculation to be wrong when a symbol precedes text.

Where to look: event label rendering in the canvas/SVG layer — likely the function that draws text inside event bars.

Acceptance criteria:
- Symbol and text are visually separated with correct spacing
- Event title text starts after the symbol, not overlapping it
- Tested across both emoji and unicode symbols in the multilane example"}
old_value: {"id":"nayra-172","title":"Fix symbol/icon overlap with event label text in multilane example","description":"In the multilane example, event bars show symbols/icons (emojis or unicode characters) that appear scrambled together with the event title text. The symbol rendering is overlapping or displacing the first character of the text label. This likely affects all events that have symbols/icons defined. Need to investigate how symbols are rendered in event labels and fix the layout so symbol and text don't overlap.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","owner":"charly.vibes@proton.me","created_at":"2026-03-03T20:54:12Z","created_by":"charly vibes","updated_at":"2026-03-03T20:54:12Z"}

Acceptance criteria:
- Buttons appear at top-right, not overlapping the timeline axis or any event content
- Buttons remain accessible regardless of timeline scroll position
- Layout tested in multilane and history examples

Verification: Load any example — buttons should be visible top-right, axis labels visible bottom, no overlap."}

new_value: Moved zoom controls from bottom-left to top-right (zoom-controls.js lines 5-6)
Implemented human-readable date formats (Jan 8, Feb '24, 2024) and collision-aware label thinning in renderer.js. Updated renderer.test.js.
Fixed in event-shapes.js, label-collision.js, search-highlight.js, renderer.js — text now starts after shape indicator with proper offset
Extended formatTime() with ordinalSuffix, Bya/Mya/kya/BCE/CE formatters. Updated renderer.test.js with 8 new tests (all 50 pass)

{"notes":"## Rule of 5 Analysis — Root Cause Investigation

### Surface Observation
Events appear as tiny dots near the bottom, with ~95% of canvas height empty.

---

### Why #1: Why do events appear tiny and at the bottom?
Events are 20px tall (EVENT_HEIGHT=20, laneSpacing=4 → 28px stride) and positioned at:
`axisY - laneHeight/2 - (lane * stride)`
where `axisY = canvasHeight - 60px`.
So lane 0 sits 10px above the bottom axis. With ~800px canvas height, that's 2.5% used.

### Why #2: Why are events all near the bottom axis?
The lane algorithm (greedy-interval-coloring) assigns events to the lowest available lane. Events that don't overlap temporally ALL go to lane 0 — right at the axis. Only overlapping events create additional lanes.

### Why #3: Why does temporal overlap determine lane count?
The vertical axis is used purely for **overlap avoidance**, not as an information dimension. Y has no semantic meaning — it's just 'where do I put this event so it doesn't hide another.'

### Why #4: Why is Y not an information dimension?
This is a standard horizontal timeline pattern. The design assumption: the primary dimension is time (X), Y is just a technical necessity to avoid visual collisions.

### Why #5 (Root Cause): Why does this feel broken in the current state?
The canvas height is the full viewport (~800px) but the event lane space needed is ~28-84px (1-3 lanes for a typical dataset). There is **no mechanism to scale lane height to fill available space**. The code has `getAdaptiveLaneConfig()` already stubbed in lane-positioning.js (line 69) with the comment: 'Future: adjust lane height based on zoom level' — the authors anticipated this need but didn't implement it.

---

## Research Findings (Codebase)

- `src/layout/lane-positioning.js`: Hardcoded `laneHeight: 24`, `laneSpacing: 4`
- `src/rendering/renderer.js:52`: `EVENT_HEIGHT = 20` (constant, never changes)
- `src/rendering/renderer.js:233`: Lane Y computed with `{ laneHeight: EVENT_HEIGHT, ...LANE_CONFIG }`
- `getAdaptiveLaneConfig()` exists but returns default config — intentionally stubbed for future work
- `fitToContent` already exists and is called on load — it fits X (time range) only, not Y

## Decision: Recommended Approach

**Dynamic lane height scaling** — single-axis change, high impact.

**Algorithm:**
```
availableHeight = canvasHeight - AXIS_BOTTOM_MARGIN - TOP_PADDING (e.g. 60px each)
maxLaneHeight = 120px  // cap so events don't become absurdly tall
dynamicLaneHeight = min(maxLaneHeight, floor(availableHeight / laneCount))
```

**Why this wins over alternatives:**
- Option A (fit-to-content): ALREADY EXISTS. Doesn't address vertical emptiness.
- Option B (swim lanes): High effort, requires semantic data (categories/people). Good future state.
- Option C (density-based Y): Hard to explain to users, requires metadata.
- Option D (compact + cards): Major redesign, breaks current interaction model.
- Option E (zoom-to-fit on load): Same as A, already implemented.
- **Option F (dynamic lane height)**: Zero new concepts, leverages `getAdaptiveLaneConfig` stub, makes the layout feel alive and responsive.

**Hybrid recommendation:**
1. **Now**: Implement dynamic lane height scaling (fills the vertical space automatically)
2. **Later**: Add semantic swim lanes (by category/tag) as an opt-in view mode

## Implementation Path
- Modify `getAdaptiveLaneConfig()` in `lane-positioning.js` to accept canvas height + lane count
- Pass computed dynamic height to `drawEvent` and spatial hash rebuild in `renderer.js`
- Cap at a max height to keep events readable (120px?)
- May need to update label positioning logic relative to event height"}

{"id":"nayra-gv2","title":"UX: Evaluate best approach to avoid empty space and small events in timeline","description":"## Problem

The current timeline view shows a lot of empty vertical space with events appearing very small at the bottom axis. See screenshot showing events clustered near the bottom (1950s-2020s range) while the entire canvas is mostly empty.

## Root Cause Analysis Needed

The issue stems from:
1. Events are positioned vertically but with no clear layout strategy to use vertical space efficiently
2. The timeline spans many decades but events cluster near the bottom
3. Vertical space is wasted — events appear as tiny dots/marks

## What to Evaluate

Consider and compare these UX approaches:

### Option A: Auto-fit / Fit-to-content
- Add a 'fit' view that zooms/pans to show only the time range that has events
- Pros: Simple, users see relevant data immediately
- Cons: May lose context of the full timeline

### Option B: Event swim lanes / row-based layout
- Stack events in horizontal rows (swim lanes) to use vertical space
- Group by category, person, or chronological clustering
- Pros: Efficient use of vertical space, scannable
- Cons: More complex layout algorithm

### Option C: Density-based vertical positioning
- Use Y-axis to represent something meaningful (importance, category, density)
- Events spread vertically based on metadata
- Pros: Information-rich
- Cons: Harder to understand without labels

### Option D: Compact timeline with event cards
- Timeline collapses to a narrow horizontal strip
- Events expand as cards above/below the axis on hover/click
- Pros: Clean, focused
- Cons: Less overview at a glance

### Option E: Zoom-to-fit on load
- On initial load or when events are added, auto-zoom to fit all events in view
- Keep manual zoom/pan available
- Pros: Quick win, minimal change
- Cons: Doesn't solve the underlying layout issue

## Decision Criteria

- Events should be visible and readable without zooming
- The UI should not have large empty areas by default
- Should feel natural and discoverable
- Should scale well with many events (10s to 100s)

## Output

Pick the best approach (or hybrid), document the design decision, and create implementation tickets.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"charly.vibes@proton.me","created_at":"2026-03-03T22:33:30Z","created_by":"charly vibes","updated_at":"2026-03-03T22:33:30Z"}

Pick the best approach (or hybrid), document the design decision, and create implementation tickets.","notes":"## Rule of 5 Analysis — Root Cause Investigation

### Surface Observation
Events appear as tiny dots near the bottom, with ~95% of canvas height empty.

---

### Why #1: Why do events appear tiny and at the bottom?
Events are 20px tall (EVENT_HEIGHT=20, laneSpacing=4 → 28px stride) and positioned at:
`axisY - laneHeight/2 - (lane * stride)`
where `axisY = canvasHeight - 60px`.
So lane 0 sits 10px above the bottom axis. With ~800px canvas height, that's 2.5% used.

### Why #2: Why are events all near the bottom axis?
The lane algorithm (greedy-interval-coloring) assigns events to the lowest available lane. Events that don't overlap temporally ALL go to lane 0 — right at the axis. Only overlapping events create additional lanes.

### Why #3: Why does temporal overlap determine lane count?
The vertical axis is used purely for **overlap avoidance**, not as an information dimension. Y has no semantic meaning — it's just 'where do I put this event so it doesn't hide another.'

### Why #4: Why is Y not an information dimension?
This is a standard horizontal timeline pattern. The design assumption: the primary dimension is time (X), Y is just a technical necessity to avoid visual collisions.

### Why #5 (Root Cause): Why does this feel broken in the current state?
The canvas height is the full viewport (~800px) but the event lane space needed is ~28-84px (1-3 lanes for a typical dataset). There is **no mechanism to scale lane height to fill available space**. The code has `getAdaptiveLaneConfig()` already stubbed in lane-positioning.js (line 69) with the comment: 'Future: adjust lane height based on zoom level' — the authors anticipated this need but didn't implement it.

---

## Research Findings (Codebase)

- `src/layout/lane-positioning.js`: Hardcoded `laneHeight: 24`, `laneSpacing: 4`
- `src/rendering/renderer.js:52`: `EVENT_HEIGHT = 20` (constant, never changes)
- `src/rendering/renderer.js:233`: Lane Y computed with `{ laneHeight: EVENT_HEIGHT, ...LANE_CONFIG }`
- `getAdaptiveLaneConfig()` exists but returns default config — intentionally stubbed for future work
- `fitToContent` already exists and is called on load — it fits X (time range) only, not Y

## Decision: Recommended Approach

**Dynamic lane height scaling** — single-axis change, high impact.

**Algorithm:**
```
availableHeight = canvasHeight - AXIS_BOTTOM_MARGIN - TOP_PADDING (e.g. 60px each)
maxLaneHeight = 120px  // cap so events don't become absurdly tall
dynamicLaneHeight = min(maxLaneHeight, floor(availableHeight / laneCount))
```

**Why this wins over alternatives:**
- Option A (fit-to-content): ALREADY EXISTS. Doesn't address vertical emptiness.
- Option B (swim lanes): High effort, requires semantic data (categories/people). Good future state.
- Option C (density-based Y): Hard to explain to users, requires metadata.
- Option D (compact + cards): Major redesign, breaks current interaction model.
- Option E (zoom-to-fit on load): Same as A, already implemented.
- **Option F (dynamic lane height)**: Zero new concepts, leverages `getAdaptiveLaneConfig` stub, makes the layout feel alive and responsive.

**Hybrid recommendation:**
1. **Now**: Implement dynamic lane height scaling (fills the vertical space automatically)
2. **Later**: Add semantic swim lanes (by category/tag) as an opt-in view mode

## Implementation Path
- Modify `getAdaptiveLaneConfig()` in `lane-positioning.js` to accept canvas height + lane count
- Pass computed dynamic height to `drawEvent` and spatial hash rebuild in `renderer.js`
- Cap at a max height to keep events readable (120px?)
- May need to update label positioning logic relative to event height","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"charly.vibes@proton.me","created_at":"2026-03-03T22:33:30Z","created_by":"charly vibes","updated_at":"2026-03-03T22:37:01Z"}

new_value: Implemented dynamic lane height scaling. Events now fill available vertical space (20px min, 120px max) via getDynamicLaneConfig(). Renderer and hit-detection in sync.
